All right, good morning or good evening or good afternoon where you are. This is the Q&A for the bridge building, the Getting Started Masterclass that we did last week. We have one question that was submitted in advance from Mary. Actually, there are a lot of questions from Mary. So we'll start there just to, it'll be our warm-up practice swings. And our general approach here is that I just read the questions because it's really my opportunity. If I read the question, it's my opportunity to decide if I don't want to be the one to answer it first because I need more time to think. So I read it and then I decide if I want Andre to answer it first so I can take some time. So that's me and transparent here. Yeah, and occasionally I feel like I'll jump into. So Mary's first question is, I don't know how to calculate lifetime value or client acquisition. This feels embarrassing, but in my current reality and something I want to change, can you recommend a resource about this? First of all, don't be embarrassed about that. I would guess 95 out of 100 people who do this professionally probably can't do those calculations accurately anyway. And there is no definitive way, frankly. I mean, customer acquisition cost, there sort of is average order value, there sort of is, but even that has some gray area. Lifetime value, certainly there is no predetermined lifetime. I mean, when I ran an agency, I had a client for 21 years, a single client for 21 years, my first client and who was also my last client. I had another for 20 years and many for 15 plus. So what was the lifetime value? Well, you don't know going into it. Other companies like the large financial publishers, Motley Fool, Agora, places like that, their business models generally are set up on a 30 day AOV. So the whole business is set up on, there's a cost to acquire the customer, 30 days to establish average order value, and then six months to establish lifetime customer life, lifetime customer value. Now, that's a very different business. And those taking that approach creates very different outcomes than saying two years, for example, we talk about Dean Jackson's research a lot, which, when you sort of know how the world works in digital marketing, you realize Dean's, like he and I are friends, Dean Jackson's data is very accurate, which is when you have a large sampling of buyers, only about 15 to 20% of the people who are eventually going to buy in the first 90 days, the remainder buy up to two years. So I like the two-year window that feels kind of right. But it's again, it's not set in stone. So there's no, you don't feel bad about this. I think the larger answer here is can you recommend a resource about this? I'm going to create that resource and put it in, we'll put it in the system somewhere, we'll do it the next week or two, just because this is important. And I use this language all the time. And I realize just because it's just part of when you work with clients, you have to be able to do that kind of calculation in your head. And you have to know how they're calculating it to understand how the numbers are going to move and the things that you have to do to make those numbers move. So I just, I can do this in my sleep, some of the few, some of the few math things I can actually do easily. So I'll do some sort of quick overview of that, drop it into, we'll drop it into the system in the next couple of weeks. Andre, anything to add to that? I know, I know numbers are your favorite thing. Yeah, I can't add so no, I don't know much to add. It's easy. Maybe your strategy is better than mine. Let me answer stuff. Done. All right. I'll just, I'll make you, I'll just hand this next one off to you instead. All right, next question. Well, it looks like there's another question before that. Okay, so that question was Mary had positioned it on the, in the A to B tension, that was the A, now that she's in the B section. So this is the background. She says, I've grown fearful of goals. I'm apprehensive of the idea of setting ambitious targets because I felt really bad when I haven't achieved them or haven't even done things. I'm not proud or have even done things I'm not proud of in order to get the set outcome. For instance, sending a dreaded last chance email, Andre's favorite, to get a certain number of sales. So I've defaulted to setting very modest and safe targets that I can for sure achieve, but then I'm not fulfilled and my business doesn't move as rapidly as it could or should. How can I overcome this fear of failure and get remotivated to play big? I can't wait to hear the answer to this because we all suffer from this. So Andre, take it away. How do we solve this problem? All right. So Mary's trying to divine B. So I think for, for us, for us, it's, it comes down to one of the things that we can control that we have full control over. So instead of just, you know, it's probably okay to have financial goals, but in the context of our A to B, the structural tension between that and then moving between the two and then once you get to B and you've done the work to convert, well, change B into your current reality, that works better for us, I think, is when you're doing something that you can fully control. So it'll be, you know, once you've defined what your priorities are, or you've prioritized some things that you need to do in order to attain whatever B is, you know, writer sequence would be something that you can control that could be part of the B. You know, again, it depends on how long it would take you to write said sequence, you know, you wouldn't want, you know, B should really be a few weeks, maybe as far out as three months, I think is what Shorinar mentioned on the call, certainly not longer than that in this definition anyway. So, you know, how long will it take you to write said sequence or build out some assets on your website that's going to help, you know, do some other component of the business, you know, create better prospects, so maybe that becomes the priority, well, how long is it going to take to do that research to write those, you know, create that thing. And I think that's how you define B. And then once you put that into the world, you've done your part, and then it's, you've done the piece that you have control over, you know, then you, traffic comes in, whether you you pay for that, or if it's, or if it's organic, but again, if it's organic, there's there's elements that you are that are outside of your control. So, if you want to control over that, you need to, you need to do activities that are going to increase, increase that, whether it's organic, you know, being on other people's podcasts and, and doing do JV deals, you know, that's those activities that you have control over, although what happens as a result of that, you don't have control over, so you can't, you can't factor that into your B. So, I think that's, that's the short answer. I don't know if, if Sean would go a different direction, but I think it's interesting. I feel really attached to this question, because I, I could have written this paragraph myself, and it would be true for me, because I've been in that same place, right? And it's that, that tension between, I want, you know, I want to dream big, you know, there are things that I really want to be true. And then, but I've, you know, it's easy to get demotivated when it doesn't happen, or it's, or if you don't, then, and then it becomes this, it's a, it's a really bad cycle, right? It's a vicious cycle where you set really big goals, you don't get there, so that you, you continue to decrease the size of your goals, so that eventually it's, they're just really easy, but then nobody wakes up every day going, hey, I can't wait to achieve this really easy goal. It just, it's not motivating. So it becomes this, it's exactly how Mary has described it, that it's, it's, it's really this, it's a different kind of tension, and it's a, it's a negative tension in a sense between, I want something amazing to be true, but I don't, I don't want to have yet another disappointment. And Andre's point, and I think this is, this is probably, you know, the way he described it is probably the best solution, which is, at least in my experience, when I define things that are, that the thing that I'm defining are outs, that's outside my actual control. And then if I don't get there, well, if it wasn't in my control anyway, how do I expect to get there? So there is a bit of a skill involved in saying, like, you know, I'll give an example for us. There was a lot of work that went into the enrollment PLS for the system earlier this year. And the actual outcome, the number of people who, who purchased the system, upgraded to the system, none of that was really within our control. The things that were within our control, the thinking behind the PLS, showing up every day to do the work, occasionally starting an email over because it wasn't, we didn't quite get there. I mean, all of that was within our control. So by focusing on that, like that, that can be very fulfilling. The challenge, I think, is to make sure that the thing that you're doing is in service to the, what you're trying to accomplish. Because when that gets fuzzy, and really, from my perspective, the central message of the, of this getting started masterclass was fuzzy stuff doesn't work. It just doesn't. Because it's easy to, to think of some of these things. Let me reword that. It's, it's sometimes more motivating to, to do activities that are, that directly contribute to, to revenue and contribute to revenue. You, you, you write a PLS, you send it out and revenue starts to come in. And those are always fun. And the activities, like those, those B activities that don't contribute directly to revenue. So, but they contribute to the potential for revenue. Some, at some point down the line, those are harder, they're harder and they are somewhat less motivating because it's not tied to revenue, which, which, which is easy to get documents squirts from. So when in the beginning of 21, 2021, when Sean had a goal of completing out of email, well, B wasn't complete out of email, because that was just insane. I mean, I'd been, I'd been trying to complete that course for years. So it was, it was silly to, to say, well, B is complete that course. So we, we, we broke it down into pieces. And our, our, us going from A to B, A to B, A to B, and that resolving tension was us creating these, these, these smaller milestones that moved us, that were that inched us forward far enough into the future where it felt like there's, there's tension there. And then we had to go and do the work and did it, but it wasn't tied to revenue. But the thing that pulled us along is once it's done, it's done this, this, this thing is, is has potential to make money for four years into the future. And that's another way to, to think of certain things that they just have to be done, but that there is not a revenue component. I'm laughing when you're writing, or when you're saying this, because I didn't, I didn't realize that's what you were doing, because at the beginning of last year, I was like, A is where we are. B is the art of emails finished. That was my, that was my A to B. Now, of course it was comical because I think at one point we had a conversation, we're like, all right, two to three months, we'll have the C wrapped up and nine months later, we, we've released it nine and a half months later. So yeah, there, there's, you know, we get, we say this a lot. There's no right way, right? There's no, none of, there's no right way. There's nothing we're saying, don't, don't ever take anything that we're saying as it's not gospel, capital G, right? This is, we, we, we think very deeply about these things and we come from very different perspectives and we, and we have a long experience of seeing certain principles hold true and at the principle level, you know, capital T truth, I think we would say we, we support this, but at, at the tactical implementation, we have to find our way as individuals and things that light some people up. You know, there are people who are just unbelievably motivated by revenue, by money, that it's not necessarily because they want to spend it or buy things, but you know, money is a neutral indicator of value and some people like to have that as a clear objective scorecard. Other people are highly demotivated by the thought of, oh, I don't want to be, I'm not in the business to make money, I want to change lives or I want to, whatever it is, but you've got to find that, that your B has to be, your, the place that's pulling you forward has to be the thing that lights you up or it just doesn't work. Right. It just, and when I say it doesn't work, I mean, the tension is not created. You know, if you say to yourself, I'm going, here I am, and I want to go to that place over there, and that place over there is not appealing, you will not be drawn to it, it will not pull you toward it. But if you take the time and ask yourself, if you say, okay, I want, in six weeks, I want to have an art, you know, a relationship building series created. And let's just say for the sake of argument that you're working on a front end for a business you have an established offer, and you want a relationship building series that leads to a hard bridge. Actually, let's, let's make it easier. It's a relationship building series has a soft bit bridge that transitions to a story powered promotion. And you need the multi page presale in front of that. Yeah, you're getting, let's say you're getting some plenty of traffic, you've got, that's all taken care of, but you need that thing. Well, you can imagine, you can create a destination postcard of having a complete multi page presale, one page, two pages, three pages, that transitions to the relationship building series that has the soft bridge transition to a story powered promotion. You can imagine what that looks like, not necessarily details, but you can imagine that being complete, and the effect it would have on your business, like what will happen when you have that. And at that point, ask yourself, does that light me up? Like if I, if that, if that were true, if I had those things, they were in place, and now I had a way that the traffic that I'm getting, I'm sending it through this thing that I've created, and I'm getting results, and I have that next step, if I can do that in six weeks, is that exciting? And if the answer is, yeah, no, okay, well, do the same exercise, but until something makes you pause and you think, hell yeah, if I had that, like that, that then that's the thing to do. Like it's the thing that you just go, yes, like that, like you feel it in your gut. If I want that to be true, because that will pull you forward. And if you get that right, in your Andre, you'll, you'll put it in, you'll make it six weeks, and you'll make it happen. If you get that right in your me, you'll say five weeks, because that shaving that week off, for whatever reason, for me, drives the action a little more for me, like that excites me, where that could be, and pay it, I mentioned that, not to contrast Andre and I, but to, to make it an additional nuance for you to consider in your toolbox, because some people, that same, that's a really good example of two subtleties that you may not pick up until you experience it yourself. Some people who set a timeframe and work to be done in the timeframe that feels reasonable are motivated by that, like I can do this in this amount of time, that feels good, let's get after it. Other people, like me, it may be a weird dysfunction, like to say, well, six weeks, I could do that in six weeks, but if I, if I knock a week off, not because I want it to get it finished a week earlier, but because I want, I want the experience that it drives when I'm right up against it, because I'm at my best when I'm right up against it. So there, there are all kinds of ways to do this. All right, we're, we went way down the rabbit hole on this one. What a great question, Mary. Wow. Okay. Next question. And now the third part. Well, I mean, I think, well, there's certainly no right way to do this and this comes down to prioritizing where you feel there's the most leverage, but then there's also nuance, you know, you need to factor in other things that's harder to measure, maybe like, you know, what do you enjoy doing? What is difficult? You know, I don't have any, like me personally, not sure. I don't have any recent experience, you know, with paid traffic. So for me, that seems difficult. Like that's, that feels like a hard thing, but creating a multi-page presale, I love that. So over the years, over the last 15 years, my default, my bias has always been to start with my sphere of influence inspired front end piece, because I have so much fun writing those and, you know, thinking up the story, what am I going to say to pull people forward? What am I going to say to repel certain people? And for me, that was always the highest reward, the highest reward in terms of the energy it gave me, the fun I had in creating it, and the bar product of that is I created, well, I create a front end that does the job that I needed to do, which is, you know, create better prospects. So as soon as people opt in at the end of that thing, they better than if I hadn't off created that. And for me, that gives me the energy to then, okay, now I'm going to write the email sequence. So that was normally my process. I started with sphere of influence inspired front end, I would then write the email, because I can write the email component pretty quickly. So that didn't take as long. The sphere of influence inspired front end would probably take the longest, but I have the most fun with that. And then email came second. And now I've got all the pieces in place to mess about with the traffic engines at that point. So that was my flow almost always. Once I built a big audience up, and I had an email list of people that, you know, essentially had their attention whenever I sent out an email. Well, in those moments, I would still create those sphere of influence inspired front ends for any campaign that I used to run. So if I was promoting something as an affiliate, part of my, so B in that case would create a little multi page presale that tells a story that then I can use my bridge email to get people to raise hand, raise their hand. And then when they raise the hand, it'll, it'll redirect to my multi page presale that exposed them to the story I wanted them to see, at which point they segmented out. And that was my process. And that was really fun. And I did that over and over and over again. But for somebody else, you know, creating that front end is maybe the most difficult, challenging thing. So it's like, well, probably don't start there then, start somewhere else. So I think that will be my answer. That's funny. I didn't, I'm glad I let you answer this first, because it took me a while to actually figure out my answer. This is such a great strategy on my part. I just love this. What occurred to me is I have a skill set that to me seems it's natural as it breathing, but it is a separate skill set. And it's because what I had to do working with clients is within a day or two, I had to be able to answer this question for them. Yeah, sometimes it was a week or more, but I had to be able to sit down with a client and ask a bunch of questions. And then from those questions, create a strategy that would move their business forward. Because ultimately, at the end of the day, that's that's the only thing that mattered. Was it my winning personality or charm? It was, did I get them results? Yes or no. So there is a skill here. And I really, I want to figure out a way to teach this system out. I'm really excited about think as I think about it. And that skill is how do you become so adept at the story of your business that this this question essentially answers itself all the time. So let me unpack that a little bit and hope that it makes a little more sense. I've said a million times, I probably said this in the masterclass too, that if you have to be able to truly understand your business, you have to be able to draw it on a piece of a single sheet of paper with a crayon. Like, where's my traffic come from? What happens when I get that traffic? And what happens as a result all the way to whatever the system is, my business system, what does it work? What is it doing? And when you become, but it's not, it's not a spreadsheet. This is where I think people go awry. They put a bunch of numbers in a spreadsheet and they're like, I still don't know what to do. And it's not, it's because this the spreadsheet doesn't tell a story. I'll use an example that just popped into my head from. This is quite a while ago, I worked for a large culinary school, doing lead gen and they were in a market of like an ultra competitive market for lead gen because they were lead aggregators. They were lead aggregator or people who buy. They use Google search and they're buying people who are looking for culinary schools in general and then they can pay a ton because they resell those leads to culinary schools. And when you're competing against them, the economics are very different because one lead for them can be sold 10, 15, 20, 30 times. Whereas for an individual culinary school, that lead needs to convert for them. So the economics are wildly different. So I was working with them in that capacity and for them it was always we just need more leads, we need more leads. But at some point I sat down and I said, okay, let's, let's look at the story that's being told here. And the story that was being told in their data was that the program that they were most interesting in building was their online program. And their online program was getting, I think it was somewhere in the neighborhood of 400 or 450 visits, unique visits, visitors per week. But they were there, the number of applicants was ridiculously low. And we were in this strategy meeting and I opened up the page and looked at the problem. It was all conversion problem. Like it was just, it didn't make, once you found that page and you found the info about the online program, it was almost impossible to submit an application. Like no one had ever actually sat down and tried to go through the whole process. And I was trying to do it in this meeting, the strategy meeting to figure out like, what's the problem here? And what was clearly a problem, the story that was being told was they're getting a lot of traffic, but they're, but the system doesn't convert that traffic in any capacity to customer. So it's a massive barrier. And their solution was more traffic. And what, and what my point was that when you have a conversion problem, more traffic is never the answer. But you have to understand that. My point isn't the specific, the point is the ability to look at your business and know the story that it's telling. And this is not easy, but it is, there's some of the dimensions that we talked about in the masterclass around, you need to understand your audience. And when we say your audience, we mean that that means, you know, your audience, if you have an email list, that's part of your audience. The audience, you have organic traffic, that's part of your audience. Maybe you do, you know, you're getting some referral traffic because you're on podcasts occasionally, that's part of your audience. Any traffic that comes to any page on the internet over which you have control, your Facebook page, let's say you have a large organic audience on Facebook, that's your audience, right? You need to understand that. And you need to have somewhere in your head, or even better, on a piece of paper to sit down and say, okay, in the last 30 days, here's what my audience looks like. I had 200, you know, let's just say I've got 500 people on a Facebook group, who I interact with in this way. And I have 10,000 people on an email list, or 1000, whatever it is, but make you have to know that. Because if you don't know, if you have no idea what the map looks like, how the hell do you know where to go? Right? And what most people do is like, oh, yeah, I know what's going on in my business. I just need to figure out what the next big thing is. It's like, okay, great, what is your cost to acquire a customer? I don't know. How many people, what percentage of people who become customers come from organic? Oh, I don't know. How many leads did you get in the last 30 days? I'm not sure. It's like, well, how the hell do you know where to go? Right? And if you don't know those basic pieces of information, and I'm not suggesting Mary doesn't, I'm just, I'm already in rant stage, which happened faster than I thought it would. But you have to know the story that your business is telling. Because once you know that story, when you do something, the story changes a little bit. Now you know something new. So let's just say for the sake of argument that the story of your business is that overwhelmingly you get organic traffic over which you don't have control. And the quality of that, the quality of the traffic is great because they consume all of your content, but you're not monetizing them. So lots of traffic, no monetization. Where's the opportunity? The opportunity is in monetization. You've got a boatload of traffic. Don't try to, because this is what happens a lot. Someone's like, well, I have a ton of organic traffic, but I'm so worried, Google's going to do this or Google's going to do that and say, great, worry about that later when that traffic is generating revenue for the opportunity when you have lots of traffic and no offer is to create an offer. The opportunity when you have no traffic in an offer is to get traffic. You have to know that story. I'm going to stop waiting. My blood pressure is already up. Anything to contribute to that before we jump into whatever is next? I'm not even sure what's next. I think that was good. Mary got to 25 minutes there. Yeah. So the contrast came through painting a picture of, of Frank who represented, who represented a portion of our audience, a large portion. And then Matt. Now Matt represented somebody who, some people would be a Matt. And then when they read this, it's like, yeah, that's, that's me. These guys are me. But it was really the, the, the pulling and the, and the repelling from, from Frank's perspective. So B in our, in our structural tension diagram, but not all Franks want to be Matt. Many, many Franks, when, when they read that and they self-identify with that, it's, it's, you know, they don't really, Matt isn't somebody that they want to go after. For them, it's that finding the shiny object and, you know, they, they landed on our, on our site and read our copy. And it's ticks that, that box the, they get the dopamine squirt and maybe they bar, maybe they refund, but it's really, they identify with, with certain traits in a way that either pulls them towards Matt or, or, or not, you know, there's, there's a choice that happens. And that's why I always kept saying, you know, well, I always say that through your multi-page presale, doesn't have to be a multi-page presale, through your, through your front-end, how you've engineered that, it's really to, to engineer this, this, this journey that somebody is pulled forward because they want to transform into this other thing. They, they are enrolled in this journey, but that's not everybody, not everybody is pulled forward in that, in that same way. There are many people, many, many people that just, you know, for them, the whole thing is, it's just about buying shiny objects, looking, looking for those magic buttons and pushing them as much as they can. And sometimes it works and sometimes it doesn't, but it's never going to be long-term. And sometimes those people need to go through that part of the journey and, and learn something from it. Like they've been on this journey, it just doesn't work after two or three years or however long it is. And then they, then there's that realization. So there was a spectrum of people that have been, that have been on that journey. And, you know, the way that that was framed resonated with, with many of those people, those Franks who wanted to be the mat or people that said, yeah, I'm exactly, I'm a, I'm a mat, I'm thinking like a mat, I just don't yet have the results. So this will help me. This is, this is the program. I'm, I'm not going to go looking for the other shiny objects. So I think how that maps with what we're busy doing now is we really, you know, mat is, is the, is the ideal customer that, that we're, that we're optimizing for. So I think that's really the beginning end of it is our audience has evolved over time. And, you know, we're going off to different varieties of mats now, but that's, you know, within that framework, I guess. Yeah, this is an interesting question. I was trying to think as you were talking, I was trying to think about, you know, how do we, how do we think about this? Like, because we're not, I don't know that we're actively repelling. It's funny. In some ways we do. I mean, the Get Rich Quick crowd, we definitely, we definitely don't. I mean, we, we, we put up a lot of road, we say just over and over. And I just, you know, it's like, got to scale bro, like, it's just, please God kill me. So I think somewhere in the back of my mind, and I, we call it out occasionally. And it's weird too. And I, I, that person, I don't, I don't want that person around. But I don't think we necessarily actively, the thing, the thing that I think we really do, and it's, it's actually, I want to give credit to Amanda, that's a bond who's on the call today, because she, she said the term the other day, I'm like, damn it, that's exactly yet, which is, I think we think in terms of like a meritocracy. Like if you take a step, like you do the work, like if you, and when we say do the work, you don't need to go make a million dollars. But if you, if you come into our world and you go through a free training, right, like you go through it, you read it and you ask us a question about it. Hell yeah. Like we'll take, we'll answer your question, we'll engage with you. But if you come into our world and you're like, hey, I, the vibe is very clear. I haven't done shit. Just tell me what I should do. It's like, hey, no, I'm not in the tell you what to do business, right? Go, go get your mom to help you with that. Right. That's not the, that's not the deal we're doing. What we're doing is we're externalizing our expertise in ways that we hope is accessible to show you what the road looks like. But you, you need to be prepared to go down the road. And the more you walk toward us, the more we'll walk toward you and the more we'll engage. So it does have that vibe of meritocracy, like the people who are doing the work. And it could be like, hey, you're on this call, you're asking questions, like that to me, that show, you've, you've, you've consumed the content, you want to improve something, you want to take steps forward, then we're right there with you. But if you want to be spoon fed things, or want us to tell you specifically what to do, wrong place. And we're, you know, we, we do have an intellectual bent to what we're doing too, which I think is a filter. It's not necessary. It's more of an implicit filter that, and we're writers. So, you know, if you want, you know, just like snack marketing snacks, tactical snacks, kind of stuff. That's not us. And it's just, we're not, we're not going to do that. Yeah, Rich asked a question to you. That's a good question to add here, which is, he says, as your business evolves, does Matt evolve? I don't think, I don't think so. I'll jump on this one first because I think we, our answer is the same, but you may have a different take on it. So it'll be fun too. So Sarah's question, what are you, what are you planning to continue with the durable business and has your plan changed from the original? So yes, definitely planning to continue. I don't, I mean, has the plan changed? Yes, but I think it's changed in a really cool way. So one of the, one of the problems with the durable business that we discovered after the fact, I think Andre may have a totally different perspective on this. But what I've, the problem I felt like we ran into was that it was, it was never going to have an end because the way that the four elements, the flywheel of the durable business, those are the, those four things are true. They change slightly over time, but they're, they're true for any business of any size. So what got confusing when once we really got into creating the durable business was, well, hell, when does this thing end? And when we put the durable business into the system with everything else, two really interesting things happened from my perspective. One, the once we hit, once you break out of that, the event horizon, that gravitational pull of the zero to up to some number, whatever income replacement number, job replacement number, 50,000, 75,000, whatever it is, once you break free of that, of that gravitational pull, then you start to see that the, the four elements continue, the four parts of that continue. But that's the point at which we really wanted people to have what we teach in sphere of influence, the art of email and the traffic engine. So we solve that problem now that they're all together, which is outstanding. The other thing that we do the other direction is that there are, there are going to be times in the durable business where you want to know more about a thing that you're working on. And like, let's just say, for example, that you validated an idea with an audience, you really have the broad strokes of it. And you're getting people to are raising their hand, or you're getting people who have, or you'll be built in enough of an email list that it's time to, to engage with them and present an offer. Well, you can go to the art of email into the PLS module, and you have that available now. So from my perspective, putting the durable business in with our courses makes our course, the other three courses so much better, because they all work on that same flywheel, which I think becomes foundational to everything. And it makes the durable business so much better, because when it's time to do the work, we can now just reference it rather than have to recreate it in, in the durable business in a light way, we can point to the wherever the sort of canonical source for that is, it's very similar to what's happened with momentum builder workshop that you can do that workshop, but realize, hmm, I really, you know, I want to have a better understanding of a bridge email when we make this soft transition in the momentum builder workshop. I want, I want to really understand that better. Great, go to go to that module in the art of email, like that's the idea. And I love, I love the durable business, I love everything about it. So I'm really excited to work on that and to really build that out. But I think to build it out, to me, that's more of a, it, it's more spread through the system than seeing it as a separate thing. And the next phase is telling you about the, the project X, which is a really, really fun thing for us because it's in the PKM space. And that just, I love that. So, I don't know, Andre, any thoughts to add to that? Yeah, it's just what Sean just said. Yeah. Why not? Do you think it's as hard as possible? That's right. That's our strategy. All right, I'll jump down to Robbie's question. This is probably an easy one for me to answer. How much are you guys incorporating paid ads into your systems? Could you run through any recent success stories? So we did, we ran paid traffic, Facebook paid traffic last year. I can't remember the exact dates. I just pulled open Wicked reports to see if I could find the dates. So for us, there's a, it's kind of, it's funny. We, so we, we turned traffic off in, I believe it was November 21st, because we weren't really, we didn't want to run traffic. We had already made the decision we were putting everything together as into the system at that point. We didn't want to, and we didn't really particularly want to run traffic in December when everybody would cost your skyrocketing because everybody's trying to advertise. We have not, we have not read, we haven't redone that yet. That's on the agenda for later. And I'll actually hit that just a little bit in a second. But the difference with us, and I want to be really, really clear about this, that we, there are two, I've run a lot of paid traffic, and there are two ways to think about it. And they're all around when, when does customer acquisition cost in average order value, when do those two things meet up? We don't have a front end offer in our business. Meaning we do not have an offer that is designed to acquire customers to offset the cost of the acquisition of those customers. Because of that, we needed to think about paid traffic entirely differently, which is we needed to take a financial risk saying we're willing to spend X amount of dollars to get however many people that brings into our world. And we know that the monetization of that, of that ad spend is going to occur essentially in two ways that we're going to pay attention to. The first is over 90 days, to what, what happens over 90 days. And because, again, we, we do what we teach. So we know that we're going to get some, somewhere in a neighborhood of 15 to 20% of the initial sales we're going to get in that first 90 days. But what we really care about and why we have to use a tool like Wicked Reports, because it's one of the few tools that will do this, is we then also need to be able to look at that group of ad spend, that money spent in the last year, and track it over two years. Because that's going to tell us, you know, it's going to show that how we're doing what we're doing, how that plays out over the appropriate timeframe, not over 30 days, we're not in a three, we're not in a 30 day optimization business. So I mentioned all of that just to be very cautious about taking any lesson from what we're doing and applying it to what you're doing, because if the underlying structure of what you're trying to accomplish is not the same, you should not take anything from how we're approaching this at all. So, you know, we have seen probably in the neighborhood of what I would have expected to see on that 90 day window. But then of course, we introduced this entire, like, we have turned the entire system on its head. So we had four core courses, we were bringing people into a relationship building series that emphasized all four of those. And then all of a sudden, we don't have four courses, and we have this one thing, so it's we've very much changed the playing field. I still expect the economics of that to work out well over the timeframe that we that we anticipated. The real change for us is going to be post, I'm guessing it's probably going to be May, maybe June, if once we get into June, it's summer, then summer sucks from a sales perspective. So I don't, I don't know what happens if we get too close to June. But what we in broad strokes, what we knew this year was going to look like is we were going to move as many people of our existing customers to the system as possible first to create a critical mass of people in the system. We've done that that happened in early February. We knew after that, the two things that we were doing, the two big things that we were doing is launching TLB lab, which that's going to happen at the end of March, March 31st through April 7th will be the actual promotion time. We'll do a PLS before that there's going to be a Founder's Discount, which is just absolutely ridiculous Founder's Discount. And at the same time, we're also doing TLB Elite, which is our high level group coaching. That's that all we planned all of that to happen first that those were the series of priorities. Once that is in place on the other side of that is where we are going to shift our focus to two tracks primarily broadly, speaking. One track is building out the system and creating the content for lab and working with our group of in Elite. That's a big track of it. The other track is we need to create a specific front end offer for our business that we can use to monetize the acquisition of customers through paid traffic. I'm sure we'll do some case study stuff about how we're thinking about that in the system. Lots of that will happen. But in the absence of having an offer that monetizes traffic, paid traffic is a different animal. That's a very different animal. So I don't know if that's the complete answer. Yeah, and see there's a lot of them chat here, wickedreports, wickedreports.com. There are alternatives. We don't have affiliate relationships, wicked reports, whatever. I know Scott and Grossilier is the Founder. He's a dear friend of mine. I've worked with him as a client for years. I know wickedreports backwards and forwards. The thing that wickedreports does that other things don't is calculate lifetime value over a very long period of time and go back and assign that value to the original source of the lead. And that's very uncommon. So we ran traffic campaigns last year that I will know the economics of for years ahead. And that is critical for our business understanding paid traffic. If your business model doesn't require that, if your business model works on a customer acquisition, a shorter timeframe, a 30-day customer acquisition window where you have a lower cost offer that you can monetize that traffic in 30 days and see it in tools like Google Analytics, great. I'm not advocating for wickedreports. When you do consider a higher level program, I happen to love wickedreports. I'm happy to talk with anybody individually about that. Not no financial relationship with them. It's good. It does what it's supposed to do. I like the fact that wickedreports was created by a data scientist. Scott is the geekiest data scientist in the world who wants to get the data. That's what he cares about. He's not a marketer. And I adore Scott. And I've told him this a million times, he's not a good marketer. He's an awesome data scientist. And that's what I want in a platform that does data science. I don't want a good marketer to tell me how great it is. I want a data scientist to give me the data. So that's my, that'll be the last thing. I can use wickedreports for brick and mortar. Yes, but I don't want to take this off on the side, changing. Yes, you can do it. Go look at wickedreports, get a demo, ask them the questions. Their demos are great. Their staff is very knowledgeable. They can answer it. And they'll be honest with you about it. Okay. Anything you want to add anything to pay traffic or? I was going to say exactly the same as you. Nice answer. All right. It's asking you for a tiny marketing agency that uses the system. Do you have any recommendations for how to get clients to engage in questions and answer sessions? For example, empathy map, chain of beliefs, questions related to world building on a level deep enough to produce answers that I can work with threats of violence. It's not about that. Bring a hammer, bring a, I'll do this from Andre first and then hand it off to you. Because I've dealt with this for two decades. I dealt with this, right? Like clients are funny. They want outcomes. In my experience, clients off the relationship between the outcomes, clients work, or sorry, the relationship between the outcome clients want and the work they're willing to do does not appear to have any correlation. It's kind of like, Hey, I want you to go do magic things. And I'm not really going to help you get there for not every client, but sometimes a large number of clients. So to specifically answer your question, I think you have to be the expert and the adult in the room. And that means, and it's a great way from positioning yourself as the expert. But to be very clear, the way you do things gets better results than the alternatives, assuming that's true. Assuming you can do the work that you have the experience and the expertise and that you can deliver. If you can say that, it's not bragging if you can do it. If you can say that, then you need to be able to sit across from a client and say, in order to get the best results for this project, I'm going to need you to engage with me on a series of whatever activities or a series of research, whatever it is. And doing that is critical to the success of this enterprise. If we just hit the high notes, we don't really get into it. We're not going to get the best result. And I will not be able to deliver the best result for you. And my integrity as a professional demands that I get the best possible result for you as my client. Like once you've made that framing, as soon as the client steps into that frame, you own the frame. So anytime the client, if the client buys into that, they're like, okay, got it, we understand. The moment that happens, anytime you meet resistance in the future, you go back to that frame. So when the client says, you know, we don't have time to do an empathy map, but we did this exercise three years ago, and I can send you the Google Doc to it. The moment you say yes to that, you're training your client not to respect what you said. That's when you have a full stop. And this takes, this is hard. And believe me, I've been in situations where I needed the client for financial reasons, and I didn't want to do this, but it will cause you endless frustration and pain. If you acquiesce this, the moment you say to the client, I need X, and they say, okay, well, we'll do this instead, it'll be just as good. You stop right then, and you go back to the frame that you set. And you say, listen, I appreciate that this work is difficult and time consuming, and that there are other things you would want to be doing. However, in the beginning of this relationship, I made it clear to you that my integrity demands I get the best results on your behalf. A three year old document that your team put together is not what I need to do my job well. I need us to do this exercise. And if you're not willing to do that, then I can't deliver the best from you, and that's not acceptable to me. So when should we schedule the time to do this exercise properly? That's what professionals do. It's scary and and you will have clients to tell you to go pound sand, right? And clients to say no. And guess what? Those are, there's a name for them. They're called former clients, right? Now, if you need the cash, and I get it, it's not always that easy. But then if you absolutely have to have the cash, and you have, and you think the relationship is salvageable, in that same situation, you can say, listen, in order to do my best work for you, I really need us to go through this exercise. If for some reason, that's truly not possible for you to do, and I need to use this, whatever, three year old document, I will look at that document and see if I can extract what I need from it. I will do my best to make that happen. But I may have additional questions for you. Who should be responsible for answering those questions? And then you just go do the work and you come back to them. But then you haven't really given up too much ground. Because the deal with clients is they will treat you the way you train them to treat you. And if you say one thing, but you're willing to, to always acquiesce to their demands, then you become the service provider who does that, and they'll, they'll drive a train through that open door. That's just human nature, doesn't make them bad people, just that's how people work. But when you, half of your job, working with clients is to train your clients to be good clients, and to hold the line. And it's hard as hell, but it's, believe me, it's worth it, especially if you're just getting started, and you want to attract good clients, because when you, when you act like that, when you're a professional, and you sit there and you show up as a professional, you attract clients who are willing to pay for professionals, because most of the world are full of dumbasses who are not professionals, especially in the service business. Every asshat in the world thinks they can go out and start a Facebook ads marketing agency tomorrow. Right. And that's, that's 80% of who you're dealing with are those people. Don't be them. Right. Be the person who says, this is what's required to do this work, right. And that's the only thing I'm going to do because I have a fiduciary responsibility to you as my client to get the best results. And you're either going to help me with that, or I'm going to go find another client. Because that's what I do. That's, that's who you want to be. Anyway, all right, Andre, you want to do the nice version of that? Like good cop, bad cop. I'm always bad cop. I'm like, I'm the, I'm the impatient cop is really what I am. The no fucks to give. We can't see that. Sorry. We'll edit that out of the transcript. I'm the no, the no something alternative to give cop. Yikes. Damn it. Right. Well, you want to add to that? Yeah, I don't really have much to say. At the end of 2019, I won't mention the clients, but a really big company approached, Sean and I to do work for them. So although it wasn't necessarily an agency type arrangement, it was us just strategizing with them. They were familiar with our work. And so they approached us within that frame. They had recognized that their style of very aggressive marketing was atrophying over, you know, and they needed, they needed to try different things. So they were completely open with coming on board and do exactly what we said that eventually turned into a bit of an agency component. But they went and did the audience not for masterclass exercises, you know, because that's part of the stuff we do. So when we said, you know, go do this, these are the questions that you need to bring back to us at the end of the week, there was no questioning us, none of that. It just went and did the work. And I think if I ever had an agency, it would be, you know, this is our expertise and this is what we do. And if you're looking for that, then that frame kind of establishes the whole front end conversation. And I recognize that most agencies aren't like that. It's a bit more messy. But when Sean and I open Elite shortly, that's essentially, you know, Sean and I have a certain bunch of expertise that is wrapped up in and codified inside of the system, you know, all our stuff that we currently do and have been doing for decades is wrapped up and codified. And it's available to everybody inside of the system. And when we open up Elite, those people, it's almost going to be, although it's not an agency thing, because we're not doing the work for them, but we're going to be helping people make sense of this and go and do the work and be part of their feedback loop when they come up against, you know, obstacles along the way, which is normal. So I think, again, that kind of answers that in a sideways sort of way. But I think if you have a certain, this is what we do. And it's very clear. And if that's not what you want, then I'm sorry, we can't take you on as a client. And that solves that problem. But again, I'm saying that for someone that's never really been in the client business. I think what I'll give everybody my favorite gift to wrap up this question to, which is, you know, we've said other places, I love questions. This is, and this is really, this is the best question in the world for clients. But it's also a great question for teams. It's a great question if you're a solopreneur. It's the best, it's my favorite question, I think. Questions are like children, though, I have so many that are favorites. This is the one that I've used so many times. The question is very simple. What are we optimizing for? So when a client comes to you, and they hire you, like, oh, you can get these results, and we've heard great things about you, excellent. And then say, okay, one of the first things we need to do is we need to create an empathy map. By the way, that noise you can hear in the background is a snowplow going by my house, there's so much snow, it's ridiculous. So if we say, all right, we're got this client, you say, okay, great, we're going to need to do this exercise and create an empathy map and we need 90 minutes from your three people on your team, and they go, oh, that's going to be hard. You know, we've got this, you've got this thing we could do. You know, we kind of did this a while ago, we can just give you that. That's a perfect opportunity to stop and say, okay, I want to make sure I understand what's happening here. I want to know exactly what in this relationship, what are we optimizing for here? Are we optimizing for this result that you've hired before, that you want to do whatever, increase sales by 20% over X, Y, Z? Are we optimizing for that, or are we optimizing for what's easy? Because the moment you pose that question to somebody, everything stops. And they're like, well, no, no, no, we want, we want this result. Great. Well, in order to get that result, we need to do the damn work. Right? Like that's the connection. There's actually a causal connection between the result you want and the activities you do. Oh my God, who knew? Right? Don't bring this attitude to the party because it pisses people off. But that's the voice that I hear in my head. And that's really, that's the question to ask all the time, all the time, what are we optimizing for? So when you sit down and there's always going to be some ass hat in the marketing department who is optimizing for their own job security and they don't want to take risks, they don't want to do anything new because they have to go to the boss and if there's a mistake or things don't perform, they're terrified. So they undermine everything that you do, or they don't want you to look better than them. There are a million things that can happen. And what are we optimizing for externalizes that stuff? Right? And I've used this, I use this all the time. I would have my, you know, I had a client for a while, that was just a consultative thing. They were running Google search and they were doing it so poorly. It was an auto parts dealer thing. And they just gave every part of the business the same amount of money. And the way that, and they, and everybody wanted to get, you know, spread that money across a million things. And it just made no sense. And then I came in and I was like, and I just did a very quick assessment. What, what, you know, the top five products were they, what are they, you know, what is the most profitable? Can we spend all of your budget within an area that you could say free, free ground shipping? The answer was yes. So they went from like advertising 200 products, only which only a few were actually selling, we reoptimized the entire, the entire account in like hours to only focus on, I think it was four, maybe five, I can't remember exactly a limited number of products that sold really well. The conversion rates were outstanding from some factors on price or whatever. We only advertised those in neighboring states where they could get it UPS ground shipping for free anyway, because UPS got there in two days. It's the second day, I guess it was like two day shipping. So their ads all said, you know, free shipping, and wasn't they didn't get an upgrade to ship and they just got it two days, but they didn't know that. And it was insanely profitable. Like few hours of work, insanely profitable. I was asked back like 60 days later, they had completely changed the entire thing back, because they the different parts of the organization didn't feel like they were getting their fair share. So they all got, you know, their fair share. And I just, and the, the owner sitting across the table for me, he goes, yeah, that ads don't work for us. And I was so irritated. I said to him, I was like, no, you don't know how to do, you don't know how to make ads work. I made ads work, you broke it. And he was like, and he was, you know, in sense, what are you talking about? I was like, I said, because you're optimizing for the wrong thing. And he sort of looked at me puzzled. I'm like, you're optimizing to make all the division heads in your business happy. So they got their fair share. And that doesn't produce good financial results. I was optimizing for financial results. I win. Right. That's the deal. You're optimizing for the wrong thing. Now, I'd love to tell the happy ending version of the story is that he saw the light and realized I was so smart. I think he called me an asshole. And I left shortly after we didn't end well, because people don't like to hear that. So he wielded this weapon with caution. But I can tell you, there is no more powerful question as a client services professional, and no more powerful question to have in the back of your mind as an effective human being than asking yourself at every opportunity, what am I optimizing for? Because I can tell you from experience, when I optimize for being right, especially if it's in a personal relationship, like with my spouse, that's not don't don't optimize for being right. Because that's not that's not the right thing to optimize for. Right. There are lots of like, figure out what you're optimizing for and make all of your decisions subordinate to that and get your clients to do the same thing. Because what you'll find with clients is what they tell you that they are optimizing for and the actual bullshit that happens, what they're really optimizing for generally have no relationship to one another. Like they'll say, we're optimizing for sales and that's it. It's a resort. We want to put heads in beds. And it's like, right, except you've got all this politics that happens behind the scenes that undermine your results. So what are we really optimizing for? Okay, deep breath. Because it's there's a wonderful book by George Leonard called Mastery. Every time someone, I've been a martial artist since I was 12, every time somebody tests for Black Belt at their testing, I give them a copy of Mastery. And what George Leonard really, when he talks about is we spend, on the path to Mastery, you spend most of your time on what he calls the plateau. You're just at this, you don't feel like you're making any progress. It's just, you know, making the donuts, one thing after the other day after day, working on your craft, and then all of a sudden, boom, you've leveled up. And it seems like it happens. Like all of a sudden, whoa, what the hell just happened? The reality is, it's the, it's the day after day that's getting you there. And then you notice that big jump. And now there's a new plateau. And like that, that's a, I have that in my mind, I think, when I think about this, the central idea that if you're, if you're doing something that's really important to you, when you're on the path to Mastery, you're going to spend the overwhelming amount of time in that making the donuts zone, where you're just doing the work. So if that's true, then if you want that experience to be positive, like, I don't, I don't want to think about things like, all right, how could we make $100,000 next month? And, and that's why I often will do things like, you know, if it takes six weeks, it's a shave a week off because that'll, that'll drive the behavior. I love that he said this in an interview that it's this idea of that we're, we're constantly building up complexity and then complexity hits a certain critical mass. And then from that complexity, you find a new simplicity and then you get that new simplicity and then it builds up to even greater complexity. And then you bring it down to simplicity again. That's what we're doing. The system right now, full disclosure is complex as hell. We've put four things together that yes, that under underlying those four things are shared principles and they play really well together. But the next evolution is to say, wow, this, this is complex. We've, we've got this large complexity. How do we take that complexity and find the elegant simplicity in it? And then we'll do that. And we'll get to this newer sense of simplicity. Doesn't mean we're going to change everything or anything, but we'll, we'll, we'll create maybe entry points or overviews or some understanding of the whole thing that's simpler and more elegant and easier to navigate.
