How do I actually calculate the metrics of a happy customer? Please proceed. Basterd. Yeah. Well, we've, we've, we've spoken about the obvious ones, minimizing refunds and, and, you know, stuff like that. But that, that doesn't really get to the essence of, of what makes a, what represents one body's a happy customer. And my thinking about this is constantly evolving. And more recently, it's evolved again. So I won't express it all here because it's still, it's, it's, you know, it's something that I've got to work with in my, in my, in my monkey mind. And eventually I'll get some, some, some clarity. But, you know, it's happy. This thing called happy customer, you know, we have a whole bunch of customers on this call now and more customers inside of our business. And I think happy for all of them are, it's going to be slightly different. But ultimately, there's probably some, some things that are common. And I think one of them is, comes down to understanding. And again, these aren't, there's, there's not a spreadsheet for this. I don't think, I don't think there is this, or there can be a spreadsheet for this other than, than the obvious thing that, that I've already mentioned. But, you know, understanding is something that's, that's nuanced. And again, understanding is going to be different for, for everyone. And everyone's coming in with, with a different worldview and a different way of, of, of not just seeing the world through their lens, but seeing their business through that lens and seeing the work that we do through that lens. And then there's these, there's, there's attention. It's, you know, any, every single one of our customers, their attention is pulled in a million different ways. You know, Andre and Sean, we just, we just two, two, two nut jobs that, that happen to be, be somewhere in your sphere of influence that you pay attention to some of the time. We would love to say you, you spend most of your attention on the work we do, but that's not reality. So I think, I think it comes down to really just understanding these, these different dimensions that represent what they, what they could be. You know, one example is, when I first myself, and my, and my original business partner with TLB one. So this is, this is back in 2011. Steve Gray and myself, when we, we first created TLB and we created a course that, that spanned over 11, seven months. So, so the lesson, so somebody would make a purchase and they would go through this training and the curriculum took 11 months because that's how we felt. There was so much stuff. It was so dense that it would be, we felt it would be a disservice to put it into, into this thing that would be accessible from day one. So that contributed to how we understood what a happy customer would be. It's somebody that we could gradually take them, you know, just like somebody goes to university, they're not given the entire curriculum, dumped on their lap and say, there we go. Exams are in six months. You know, go do your best. You go through this thing and it's gradual. And the reason is because of, you know, understanding plays out in that way. So that's why we did that. And then it's not weird. We, we got to the end of, of the training and then we surveyed all of our customers and those that responded. The large majority, maybe the large majority is overstating it, but there was a, there was a significant portion of people that were still stuck on like module one, which was how to find your, your niche. You know, that's, that's the wording that we, that we used back then. It's your, your, this thing that you're going to go and do and create in the world and this, and these, and the segment of people that you're going to go after. And these are your people. Well, people were stuck on that, that part was so difficult for them that the other six months of the training almost became irrelevant. And we only, we only realized that after the fact, right at the end. So, although we were with the best of intentions, we were trying to, we were trying to engineer a way to disseminate our training in a way that we were going to create a happy customer. We didn't create a happy customer for many of them is because they were still stuck right in the very beginning. And then nothing else mattered. So it's just, it's things like that. It's, it's understanding the nuances of a market. And that is not easy to, to really wrap your head around because there's just so many dimensions to it. So I'm not being specific on purpose because there is, you can't shine a lot on this thing. It's just a feeling. There needs to be a feedback group where you're interacting with your customers, you know, do, does the attention you have that you command with those customers, is that atrophying over time? And, you know, there's obviously going to be some level of atrophy, but, you know, what is it? I mean, I suppose you, that's can be measured, measured in some way. But yeah, it's just trying to define what those, what those dimensions are, and then really doing everything to try and, and, and trying to make those, those, those, those elements more accessible. And understanding is one. I mean, we'll, we'll be doing everybody a disservice if we just lump this on everyone. There's a reason why we're doing this Q&A call, right? It's, it's because we're trying to learn or understand what don't you understand. So then we can then go and put it inside there. And because without the level of understanding with any of our courses, let alone this thing called the system that we still busy trying to articulate. If somebody just is exposed to this and literally shit their pants, I'm sorry about the, the, the, the, the visual there, but they, they just kind of bounce straight away. And although they may not refund because the level, the, the, the perception of the stuff that they've just bought, that level of, of awesomeness, it's, it's clear it's there. And that person may not refund. So therefore you, you quite even measure that element. But it just becomes, we just become another statistic on the, on the virtual bookshelf. And over time, maybe even a short period of time, we just, we just are faulted out of that person's attention to the point where even though they haven't unsubscribed, even though they haven't refunded, we have, we have lost the ability to communicate with them in a way that's meaningful. And that's, that's a disservice. So that's again, that's represents somebody that's not a happy customer. So I'm sorry if I'm, if I'm skirting around the issue, but it is complicated. And I think it's just, it's just understanding that it is complicated and that you do your absolutely best, you're absolutely best to try and constantly increase the experience that somebody has with, with, with this course that they've purchased or this thing that they've, the, the skill that they want to get better at. And on the other side of that, on the other side of the understanding is the doing component. And part of the doing component is getting a result. And these breakdowns can happen at any point. Somebody can like conceptually get this, I love, you know, out of email, I understand this. And then they stare at their business. And there's this fear that, oh my God, I've got to change everything, what I change. And that, that paralysis that comes from, from that fear, that's also us doing a disservice. Because again, we can't be responsible for somebody else's outcome, but we can be responsible, responsible to try and make it so accessible that it becomes a no brainer to, to take it and use it. And ultimately try and get a result from it. And I think the ultimate, you know, checkbox for happy customer is somebody that's understood enough of the, the, the, the training that matters to them. So obviously, the, the system is made up of a whole bunch of parts and, and this all comes together in this systematic thing. And people are going to be interacting with, with different components, and they are going to choose certain things and put it in their business. That makes sense and get a result. And if that's a positive result, now we're moving in the direction of happy customer. And I think out of all of our customers that have stayed with us the longest, and we still have their attention, are the ones that have understood enough and then gone and put it into their business, even if it's messy, and got a result. And then they just, that becomes a feedback group. And they just run on that little, on that flywheel. And we stay front and center as somebody worth paying attention to, because they can see that this stuff works. So I think that's my, that's my longest answer. I appreciate you making me feel better about saying fuck earlier. Thank you. It's, I, I so love this question because this is a play, every, I know everyone thinks that we just, you know, well, I think a lot of people think I'm actually Tyler Durden, and it's like, I'm just the alter ego and not a real person. So this is going to be, maybe this is all engineered to, but this is a place we, we really disagree, I think, in many ways. I love that we finally get an opportunity for people to realize that we disagree about a lot. So I'll come out, happy customers from a slightly different perspective. And I'll draw attention to one of the things that, that anonymous, aka Jason Bourne mentioned, which is how do I actually calculate the metrics of a happy customer? Because my fear with the happy customer thing always is people are like, well, should I don't, I don't know, I don't know how to put all these parameters into a spreadsheet. So I just won't do it. Right. And I was like, no, don't, don't, it's not an excuse not to do things. So the way I think about it from a metrics perspective is because, because this, this becomes actionable is it's simple. It's customer, the, the, the gateway to becoming a happy customer is you have to be a customer, which means you, you don't refund. And the reason, and it's, it's incomplete, but from a metrics perspective, what you need to know that can, that can change your behavior is that's the only thing metrics are supposed to do. They tell you a story that, that informs your future behavior, that there's no other reason to look at metrics if it's not going to change your behavior. That's why I don't give a shit about when someone's like, well, what percentage of our audience does XYZ? I don't care. It's not actionable. Right. So actionable metrics. If you know that you're generating 100 customers a month and 30 days later or 60 days later, whatever your refund period is, 60 days later, you have 50 customers who stayed. So you have about 50% refund rate. You get a problem. Right. And if, if it's, so then it becomes actionable where you're like, whoa, maybe, maybe I've turned the persuasion dial a little too high. So, and let's look at some comparisons, right. So let's say you're doing a hundred, you get a hundred order, you're just hardcore countdown timers, bonus stacking. I mean, you name it, you've gone through, you've, you've, you've gone through the best and brightest offer guys on the, in women on the internet. And you've just, you've got it all there. But, and you're, you can convert people like a machine and you're getting a hundred, you know, your conversion rates are stupid high. But after, but your product sucks, your support, whatever the experience sucks or not, or just, you're just, you're just really good at selling and you get a hundred purchases a month and then 50% of them go away. So you pull a bunch of it so that you're left with 50. Right. But then you dial down a bunch of that nonsense and you only get 80 sales. Well, is that better or worse? Well, who the hell knows? Right. Most people are going to say, Oh my God, my business sucks. I went from 100 sales to 80. I'm losing ground. Well, what if those 80 sales, 65 of them are still customers at the end of the month? Is your business better or worse? It's a hell of a lot better. Right. So you have to know, you have to have a metric that's set in the metric. And let's, let's not call it, let's actually, let's make it easier and not call it happy customers. Let's call it, I don't know, customers. Like, because you really don't, you know, actually, the way I would distinguish this is I would call people who buy buyers and people who are there after day 61 customers, right. They've, they've lasted. They're your customers. And that's the metric I care about. And, and yes, I love all the others, not all the other stuff. I love most of the other stuff. I love things about like, how engaged is the audience with communication? Do people like you show up on call? Like all of that, that's all that all matters. But what doesn't matter as much to me is I'm less concerned about consumption metrics. And I'm way less concerned. This is going to sound like such an ass. That's all right. This is all about honesty. We're all friends here. I'm also less concerned about execution metrics. But all of a sudden, my street cred's gone. Let me tell you why. First of all, people buy things. We teach this. People buy things for any number of reasons. I buy tons of stuff. I don't do anything with it. And I'm still glad I bought it. Sometimes people buy it like they buy our stuff, because they're buying hope. They bought this thing that they hope someday is going to turn everything around for it. And they never act on it, but they feel good because they've got that hope. If buying something that we sell makes somebody feel good, but I don't like the way it makes them feel good. Who the hell am I to say they can't feel good about it? Bullshit. Not my job. Their job. So someone drops, someone walks in today, never heard of us, who says, these guys look great. I want to be an internet marketing whatever. I want to build my business. I'm going to do this thing. Oh, yeah, here's $1,500. And they never log in. But every time they think about it, they feel good about it. Like, oh, yeah, next month, I'm going to hit that hard. I bought that. I'm going to nail that. I'm going to get that next month. It's not up to me to pass judgment. You do, you all do me. And on the consumption metric side, where I get wound to is our job, I think, is to be in 100% integrity to say, from our experience, we're 40 plus years, we're 43 years between the two of us. And we think really deeply about this stuff. It is our job to put out the best possible explanation of what we believe works and why. And to constantly test that and to constantly smash it up against situations and constantly refine it. And the customer's job is to figure out how to get the most from that. And getting back to Amanda's comment about meritocracy, I think I have a real strong meritocracy, like component of my DNA, that I am not going to hold your hand and walk you through everything. And I'm not doing that. Because now that doesn't mean I don't want to engage with you. I do because I love it. But I'm not going to hold your hand and say, okay, little Johnny, I'm sorry, I'm going to say John's call. Okay, little Johnny, it seems like you're struggling to get through this stuff because parentheses, you're not really taking the time to do it. But let me let me take you by the hand and make sure you're happy. Bullshit. Do the work. And when you do the work, come like raise your hand. It's like, all right, I don't understand what you meant here. I'm trying to do this thing. And it doesn't make sense. Okay, let me then it's my job to make it make sense to you. But I'm not in the, it sounds like, and I'm making this more extreme because it's really not that far apart of this. But I'm trying to make it make our differences like broader that I don't all the things about do you go through like do they go through it and do they do the work? Did they get a result? Do they solve their problem? That's on them. That's on the customer. I'm from my perspective, because we all have our jobs to do. And the reality from my perspective is the easier I make it for somebody. The lower the less satisfying the outcome will be, I think, maybe I'm wrong. I'm just seeing it through the lens of my own experience. But it certainly there's a martial arts component of this too in my experience where students who just progress through the ranks easily tend to be far less satisfied than the students who work at it and get pushed who struggle and feel like they accomplish things. Maybe that's the wrong metaphor. But I think there's something to that where I want I want the people who do the work to rise up in their actions. And those are the people who when they raise their hand and say, could you help me with this? It's, it's, hell yes, I want to help you with it because I see you're doing something. And I want to help you do that because you're my people, right? You're doing it. But the, and at the same time, the person who buys the course endables but feels good about it. And is excited about it. It's positive. That to me is a happy customer. Like I just, they get to decide what makes them happy. So to summarize, you know, from a, I do think you have to have a metric is if you don't, if you have to have a metric that's actionable. And from my perspective, that's buyer and customer with no emotional judgment around whether or not they're happy because I don't know day to day if I'm happy. I don't even know day to day if I care. I don't know, you know, who knows? Like happy is usually a retroactive thing. But where I think Andre and I are 100% in line is that we use happy customer as a frame for every decision that we make. For example, we initially intended to anyone who had three or more of our core courses, we initially intended to just upgrade them automatically to the system. Right. And we charged it a nominal fee, $95, not because we wanted to make the $95, but because that actually filtered out about 70% of the people who didn't even bother to raise their hand. It made some, it made a segment of our audience have to do something to take a step closer to us because we believe that's likely to make a happier customer. That at the end of the day, not getting it for free, having to actually do just something made the difference. And we use this all the time in our thinking. So separate the idea of, I guess, three ideas. One, happy customer is a framing tool. Like that, that's one definition of happy customer. Happy customer as a metric, which is buyer versus customer after 60 days or 30 days. And then this third component is, it's a term I heard from Andrew Huberman the other day, why I love, which is anecdote, meaning it's anecdotes that you're sort of mistaking for data, that anecdote are things like send out an email, we get a bunch of replies. They're not statistically significant, but they're important. Or we do a call like this, we get a certain number of participants, not statistically significant, but important. They inform our decision making in sort of a texture way. They help us know what resonates, what doesn't, where we're getting it right and where we're not. But we're not fooling ourselves into thinking their metrics. And but we're also not saying it's too complicated to have a metric because you need the metric between buyer and customer to because that informs better decision making. Okay, that was fun. So read it. All right. I think I'll come at this a different way, which is we've written about this a little bit, but it's really foundational to a lot of successes I've had broadly defined, which is it's an idea from Charlie Munger about the importance of avoiding mistakes versus trying to be innovative or smart. So I'll be crystal clear. I was never innovative when I ran an agency, especially on the traffic side of things. Nothing I did was like cutting edge at all. The thing that made me so successful, I didn't know it at the time. It wasn't until I think MFA live, I mentioned it on stage and I realized like I heard myself say it, I was like, oh yeah, that was it. But what made me very successful on my client's behalf was that there are 10, 15 or so at the time, I had a list of 12 very specific mistakes that people made primarily, I was talking about this in the past, it was primarily around Google ads. There were 12 mistakes that were very common. And I wasn't innovative, but my clients just didn't make those 12 mistakes. And if you don't make those mistakes, and there's a great metaphor from this, which is you can't out exercise a box of donuts, meaning you can be the most jacked crossfitter in the world. Go to the, I won't call it a gym, because I know your crossfitters call up the box, go to the box like five days a week, smash it with your high intensity workouts, high five each other, yeah, bro, smash it, smash it, smash it. And after every time you go to the CrossFit box, you eat a half dozen donuts, guess what? You're not ever going to out exercise the mistake you're making with the donuts. So that's mistake avoidance. Charlie Munger, who's Warren Buffett's business partner, describes it, there's a book written in his honor about this one idea that summarized it perfectly, which is, all I ever want to know is where I'm going to die. So I never go there. Right? Like, that's, that's the brilliance of this idea. So, and it's, it partly comes from inversion, which is a, I think Jacobi is the person who's the idea. It's like, instead of trying to figure out how to do X, you try to figure out what would be involved in non X, and then you never do that. Right? So most people will come out and say, like, oh, what, you know, what five steps should we take to have the, you know, the most effective business in 2022? Right? And everyone comes up with ideas. Another way to do it is to say, hey, let's figure out, let's make a list of like all the things we could do that would really undermine our success this year and take it serious. Like sit down, like, oh, we can have really crappy customer support. We could, you know, like, like make the list and then just commit to never doing any of that. Right? It sounds like it's the same thing, but it's not. And I don't know why it's not. There's something very different about mistake avoidance when you do it right about inversion, when you do it right, that you get a result that you don't get the other way. I don't know if it's how our minds are situated. I don't know. I don't understand it, but there's something really weird when you sit down and ask yourself, it's like, it's a weird permission. Like, I'm sure if I, like, I would love to do this, the conversation with Amanda, like if I sat down, I was like, hey, Amanda, tell me, tell me all, like, minimum 10 things that I could do that would really screw up my Facebook advertising results. Like, and, and, like, really think about it and ask me, you know, some questions, make my list and you really refine it. I could probably make a, let's just assume that we come up with 10. I can make a list of 10 things that if I just didn't do those 10 things, I would probably be way ahead of my competitors who are likely doing some or all of those things. Right. That's how inversion works. So, I mean, you can go to Farnham Street, Shane Parrish's blog, he has some articles on lots of stuff on Charlie Munger, lots of, there's a great interview with a guy who wrote one of my favorite books, which is, it's his name is Peter Bevelin. There's a great interview with Peter Bevelin about this idea of mistake avoidance because, you know, in Andre and I talked about this earlier this year at the end of last year about, it's not about trying to be smart, it's about trying to be less dumb, and it sounds like we're being humble, but we don't mean it. Like, I thought it's not humility, it's not false humility, it's just recognizing, there's so much we don't know and it's just, it's all, it's a, each day is an opportunity to be just a little bit less dumb about different things. If that feels different, there's a different energy around that. Maybe that doesn't motivate you. You know, some people are far more motivated by the positive than they're moving towards something. And I tend to be more motivated away from like not wanting, for me, I'm motivated by not wanting to be dumb. And, and I'm dumb in so many ways and in such, so thoroughly dumb. And if you want to feel dumb, like really, in this, I don't want to be like, why would I want to feel dumb? Because there's no judgment in it, right? Because I don't, when I say I feel dumb, I don't feel bad about myself. I'm not, I'm like, you're a, you're a jerk. Oh, I, I just, I know they're whole, they're whole worlds. I'm not, first of all, I'm not even aware of. So it's not knowing, do I not know what I don't know? I don't even know that I don't know it. But if you want to, if you want to feel as dumb as I do, go listen to Michael, to Sam Harris, listen to the audio book, but it's Sam Harris' book, Making Sense. It's 22 hours of his podcast with some of the biggest thinkers around some of the most complicated topics. And it's just, it's just moment after moment of, I don't, oh my God, I don't even know what they're talking about. Like, I don't even know, and it's beautiful. It's just a wonderful experience. So that's a, that's tangential to the question that you asked. But it's, it's, that idea has, has really improved the quality of my life well beyond any measurement I could ever give it is just to ask myself in any situation, what are the, how could I get this really wrong? And just when I'm, when I'm on my, this is personal, this is relationship stuff, this is professional, this is creative output, it's everything, but just to, just to identify early, how could I get this really wrong? And then just keep that in mind, don't do that. Because then if I just, if I don't do that, at least my results aren't going to be terrible. Right? I don't know. That's, that would be my, my contribution. Okay. Next one. Alexandros, I hope I'm pronouncing that correctly. In the spirit of happy customers, uh-oh, customers, in the spirit of happy customers and no hard persuasion tactics, would you consider entirely removing the money back guarantee? Oh, this is going to be a fun one. This happens only in the digital world. You don't ask your money back if you don't like to yogurt you got from the supermarket, right? All right, Andre, what do you think? Because we've thought, we've thought about this. The first, I think the, certainly the first time in our partnership that we ever referenced the money back guarantee was for the system. I don't believe we've ever mentioned that explicitly in the past. So I'm, I'm guessing that you didn't ever mention that in the past for a reason. So I'll let you, why don't you take this and then I'll, my answer to why I think we did this is very straightforward. So take it away. Uh, yeah, I mean, you know, the guarantees are guaranteed, right? So it's just relieving some, some unknown because buying a $1500 product or $500 product is not the same as buying a yogurt for $150, you know, that's different. So if there was no money back guarantees, I think it would certainly suppress. Now, that's not necessarily good or bad because, you know, someone that really feels that, you know, based on what's, what's already been shared, these guys are talking, they're talking my language. I'm going to get this thing, regardless of the money back guarantee. If it's even, I mean, because we don't mention them on, on our pages. We do it occasionally inside of the emails, like in the last email, I don't forget to get a money back guarantee, but very few people have actually reached out to us because we haven't got the money back guarantee on the, on the site anywhere where there was, hey, I'm just, just want to make sure, you know, do you guys offer it? And then, you know, let me say yes. So, you know, one of the experiments I wanted to run a few years ago was, was make the money back guarantee explicit. So I would have it front and center, but it would be, it would either be a one year one, or a one, or an unlimited one. So I would explicitly say, this is an unlimited guarantee. If you want to, if you need your money back, in five years time, I will refund you that money. I never got around to doing it. I don't know what would have happened, but we use Clickbank for our front end courses and course now. And they, they do enforce money back guarantee. That's, that's something that you have to, you have to offer. So it's, it's always, it's always on the table. And in fact, even if we didn't have a money back guarantee, so we're going to be using Strap, or the plan is to use Strap for, for our continuity when we get that online with, with lab and elite. So we don't have to offer a money back guarantee. And even if we never do and it's never, it's never mentioned anywhere, we never mentioned it in the emails. If anybody ever came to us and say, Hey, I would like my money back, we would get the money back. They would, they would never be an issue, at least from our perspective, I think Sean, Sean, Sean thinks the same way. But yeah, you know, I think that's me skirting around the question. But it's, it's funny too, because I, I started, as you were talking, I started to make my answer far more nuanced than it actually is. I was like, yeah, I was listening to you and I was like, Oh yeah, but the reality is when I, when I thought about it from a decision making perspective, it wasn't nearly as nuanced as I'm thinking about it now that we're being asked the question. So it would be dishonest to suggest it was this nuance. But for me, it came down to something very simple. Clickbank is our, is the platform on which we make sales, clickbank require, clickbank has a 60 day money back guarantee thing, like we can't, we can't deny anybody who requests a refund through clickbank in 60 days, that's part of the deal of using your platform. So it seemed foolish to me that we don't just say that for a couple of reasons. One, we have to do it anyway, so we might as well say it, but two, I think it, and this is the part I'm not, I don't remember specifically having this thought. So I may have, I want to think that I did. But I wanted to be clear to people when they buy that if they come to us and they want a refund, that's perfectly cool, like come to like, it's almost like if we didn't say it, then it's kind of like, well, then they just go to clickbank and like kind of like, they don't want to say something. It's like, no, like if it doesn't, if it doesn't work for you, if you buy it and it's not what you want, just let us know, like, it's cool, no worries, like let us know, we'll take care of it. Like, I wanted people to feel comfortable with that. Any of whether or not it's, you know, it's the question is framed, you know, happy customers, no hard persuasion, implicit in that is that there's a possibility that the money back guarantee is falls under the category of hard persuasion tactics, like that's implicit in this question. And I think the way I would answer that question, it's a quote I love from Zig Ziglar, I'm not a big student of Zig Ziglar. And I don't, I don't know enough about, I don't know who he was and other things, but there's something Zig Ziglar said that many others, Jay Abraham and others have really expanded on. But the original quote from Zig Ziglar is that if you believe your product or service can fulfill a true need, it's your moral obligation to sell it. Now that's like, I think about that a lot, because we both embrace the strategy of preeminence, Jay Abraham strategy of preeminence. And when you, when you take it to its logical conclusion, it's explicit logical conclusion, as described by Jay Abraham, you, you have to, there's a part missing in the description that I think we've, we've filled in, which is important that I'll fill it, which is you, when you, if you truly believe that you're, and you're not fooling yourself, but if you're, if you're putting your heart and soul into something, and you believe and you have evidence to support, that this truly works, it truly fulfills a need, it truly can change people's lives if they do their part. I do believe you have some responsibility to, for the people who should have it to have it. Now, where do we draw the line and you know, we don't love the hard persuasion stuff, but make no mistake, we still persuade, right? We're not, we're not reading the books on persuasion, we're not using those tactics, but we write in ways that are pulling the right people toward that outcome. That's, we're deliberately, because we believe what we've created changes lives. If we didn't, then all bets are off, right? Like then you cross into the unethical side of things, but, but then the ethics are around the crappy product, not how you sell it. Like it's, it's, it's not the selling, it's that everything's a system, right? It's, it's the relationship. So if you, if you have a really good product, and you've got the right audience in front of that product, and, and you pull out all the stops to sell it, is that okay? I feel pretty good about that. But if the product is crappy, and the, the audience is wrong, and those parts aren't synergistically lining up, then, then you can't apply the same logic in the same statement. So I, I, I think I land on this of being, it's not persuasion in the, in the service of the buyer's best interest is okay with me. But never, but we, we feel, we feel it when it's off, and we don't go there, if it feels like too much. And the money, money back guarantee didn't feel like too much because we felt like more than anything, it was something we had to do that we were making people aware of that was available to them. And I wanted them to feel like if they did take the chance and go, you know what, this was not for me, that everything was okay. They could just let us know. And no problem. Like, it's not a big deal because it isn't. So long answer. Damn. Yeah, we're not going to do, we're not going to end at 10 today, apparently. Okay, we should probably try to go faster with some of these, which has so much fun. And I love these calls. All right, Robbie. What's your view on creating affiliate partnerships early on in the startup? Do you leverage any affiliate networks to drive traffic to your own systems? I want you to answer that. And I will, you answer that. And I'm just going to go on to the next one after you're done answering it. Yeah, we, we don't leverage affiliate networks. In fact, we're, we're, we've manually turned ourselves. So, because we're on the clickbank marketplace, well, we, we sell through clickbank, there are, there are retailer. So they've got a huge marketplace where you can go and search for, for, for products. So if you're an affiliate looking, looking for offers to promote, you can go to the clickbank marketplace, do a search under a certain category, and all the things come up, it's got the conversion rates and all that stuff. And then most, most, most affiliates will then just sort by the top three products that make the most money and off they go, and they just throw traffic at it. So for that reason, we're, we're unavailable on that network because we don't want junk traffic. And I think you can build a business that can make a lot of money from shitty traffic from affiliate networks, and many people have got rich from that. It's, you know, make no mistake, it's a full-time job. And there's elements of it being, you know, gray, unethical, because it's just, it's just, it's just pimping bits. You know, it's, it's not about relationships. So I think that's our thinking about that. We would rather handpick audiences. So we'll reach out to certain people like, like Commander Bond, for example, you know, if her audience is a really good fit for our stuff, well, then we will, we'll, you know, do a deal with, with Amanda. And that's, that's win-win for both of us. And she endorses our stuff and we all make money and we, we get really good prospects coming in and really good customers coming in. And that's more meaningful for us. But I don't think you found that on, I'm generalizing here, but I don't think you, you can find that on affiliate networks, unless your offer is very broad, like make money online, broad, like click buttons and you're going to get rich tomorrow, broad, then you can go to the affiliate networks. And again, that's just a different game. And that's, that's not the game we run. So we don't. Sorry, I'm typing away at the answering Karma's question. Oh, all right. Let me, we'll just move on to the next one. How far under the durable business would you go after feeling like your ideas are quite right? Are there any examples you can give of starting business ideas and taking them too far or stopping too early? I mean, it's, it's data, right? I mean, it's, it's mostly data. I mean, if you, if you're, if you're killing yourself, why kill yourself to make it something work that's not working? If you go in, you get a tepid response from an audience. I mean, first of all, you should never get there. Like you, you should know before like the whole, the whole reason to validate an offer in the first place is so that you don't go out into the marketplace with an offer that underperforms. So you, by the time the offer is made, the work should have been done for that offer to knock it out of the park. Because you should have heard from people that there's a real need. You should have data from, from responses to the way you formulated and frame things that suggest people are really interested. You should have had conversations, whether electronically, asynchronously or synchronously zoom, Skype, something else with people and heard their language and in heard the intensity of it and really have a sense that they're on boards. Like all of that, all that should have happened so that you shouldn't find yourself in a place where you're like, it just doesn't seem to be working. Like just, most stuff's not going to work. Like you're going to find that out early, but be clear. It's offers that don't work. It's not, and it offers an expression of an idea. It's not the idea itself. And so it may be like, you may have this idea that you want to do a one-on-one coaching XYZ and it tanks because the market wants small group, right? Like, or the market wants DIY. It doesn't mean that the knowledge you have to share isn't desirable. It just means the expression of how you, now it may very well be the knowledge you have, but you don't know that yet. Let's just phrase the absence of evidence. It's not the evidence of absence, right? So if you don't have any data that says specifically that this isn't working, you can't assume that it's not working. You have to go out and run the experiments. So yeah, I don't know if you want to add anything to that. Yeah, but my perspective is that competition, it validates ideas. So when you look around, so if you look at our little audience and often in economics circle framework, that audience component, if sure enough, are getting off to the PKM space. So this is for project X, for example. Well, we can look in any which way we look, there's people making money or selling stuff in the PKM space. So that audience component is ticked. We don't have to ever wonder, I wonder if this audience spends money. Well, they do. So the only other thing that's left is the offer component now. As Sean said, it's an expression of an idea. Now, that one's idea could be shit. And then normally are, you normally got to iterate towards a good idea and also expressing that idea in a way that within that audience pool of people making money in that space is expressing that offer in a way that talks to a certain segment of people in a way that pulls them forward, that you become a little category of one, all those things. That is the iteration in that offer box where you try to express an idea in a way. And so that's where you end up spending most of your time inside of the general business iterating there. You know the audience is good. This isn't about in the old days, what niche you'll go after and it really people's thinking behind that they're implicit in that was, we're going to make the money, we're going to make the money. It was less about building a business that they want to do long term. So if you went when Frank Kern did the whole underachiever thing and you're going to build 50 little businesses each making a thousand a month in these obscure markets. Now that's different. If you're into hamster breeding secrets, well maybe there's not enough people that are interested in buying hamster breeding secrets. So in those examples you can get audience wrong all day long because there's always this unknown. But today on the internet where there's competition everywhere you look and that competition validates markets and that's a good thing. And so that audience box can be ticked off very quickly. But what's important and what we try to express inside of the general business is that audience component really better be something that you want to do and you can see yourself doing long term. It needs to light you up. It shouldn't be just a money making venture. Money obviously is important that economics because hobbies are hobbies and if you're not going to make any money from it and you want it to replace a day job, well it better make money. But that audience thing, it really needs to be in something that you enjoy doing. So therefore when you understand all of that the only thing really, the biggest thing that's an unknown early on is the offer component and an expression of that idea. And that's normal. But that's not starting again. That's not scrapping the entire thing. This PKM thing is just rubbish. Because ARCOR makes money in the PKM space does not mean people aren't making money in the PKM space, which they clearly are. So yeah, I think I'm going around in circles now but I think that's how you're just iterative there. Next question is from anonymous, aka Jason Bourne, should I create a relationship building sequence and then a multi-page pre-sell before I do awareness stuff? What's the real way of doing stuff? Here's some tough law of anonymous. Go to the durable business and go through that content. Because it answers this question. And this is like how long is a piece of string? There's no clue how to answer this question with such limited information. If you're at zero, then the answer is go to the durable business. Because you've got a lot of work to do before you start thinking about building a relationship building sequence or a multi-page pre-sell. Because who knows what it's about? Now, if you have an established business with an offer that converts and a long history of that converting and you don't have a relationship building sequence and you don't have a multi-page pre-sell and you have both tons of traffic, maybe that's a good idea. But without, you just can't answer this question. It's impossible. And it's, think in terms of, like, start with the context. I have X, then, you know, with X. Again, this is the central point of this master class, which is establish the parameters of what current reality is for you around your offer or offers, around your audience or audiences, the economics of what you're doing, and the experience of your business. Construct a similar or construct a future state using those same parameters. Understand the delta, the difference, the time frame and the difference between those two things. And that's going to tell you what work needs to be done. But without knowing what your A is, and the answers to those questions, we have, we can't possibly tell you whether or not. And in this last question, this is a fascinating question. What is the real way of doing stuff? The real way of doing this stuff is exactly what we said in the master class. That's exactly how to do it. It's why we said it. It's why it's in the master class, right? Like, sit down and figure out the answers to those questions and figure out where you are right now. Then figure out where you want to go. Once you've done that, ask yourself if a relationship building sequence and a multi-page pre-sell are the priorities to get you there. That's how you answer this question. It's not like I'm being a jerk. I'm not mean to be a jerk. I'm trying to do this little tough love here, that that's how you answer the question for yourself. That's the real way of doing this stuff. Andre, you want to add a kinder, gentler version to that? Or are you okay with my bad cop version? I love bad cop, yeah. All right, move on to the next one. All right, this is from Paul. From your experience of working with clients, has there been a shift in how the business community sees more model of marketing versus the more traditional direct response model? You hinted at this earlier. Do you want to take it from the respect to some of the client work that we did? Yeah, again, I don't have a deep experience with clients, but one of them which runs a really, really large publishing business that does very aggressive, traditional direct response marketing, things are changing and they're changing in ways that certain companies aren't prepared for. So I think, well, I think that was always going to happen. It was just when. So you can only be aggressive for, like really aggressive for so long and then things just need to change if that's the business you're in. And some companies, this is neither right or wrong or indifferent. It's just some certain business models. It's always whatever is at the edge of what I can get away with. They will build business models as far out as possible. And then there's other businesses in these same spaces that are very that are very competitive that will have a different version. So that will do a less aggressive version. And then their businesses will be completely different and how they interact with the customers will be completely different. It's just different ways of doing it. And I think maybe again, not only generalize now, but perhaps there's more people coming around to our expression of modern marketing as we've that we attend to externalize and start up the system. Yeah, I think I'll leave it at that. Yeah, I can't remember where we talked about this, but we have, which is the the short answer is yes, because the economics of traffic change so fundamentally that the conversion rates that used to work no longer work. So back in the day, when you could get one cent clicks on Google all day long, then you could have your business model could work when you have a lot of cheap traffic. But there really isn't any cheap traffic anymore. Those days have kind of gone. I mean, you can get less expensive traffic, but the one cent click days are over and they're not coming back. So and traffic costs have risen steadily over time. And to be clear, when I say traffic costs, most people are paying, most people pay for traffic at some stage. And most large businesses pay for essentially all of their traffic, because it's predictable and it's dependable. So if you're getting a ton of organic traffic, you're like, what is he talking about traffic costs? Well, the game being played by most of the big players, you have to pay for traffic because you just need a lot of it. And when it's not cheap anymore, 1%, half percent quarter percent conversion rates don't work. And that has had seismic repercussions throughout the internet marketing world that as traffic becomes more expensive, you need much higher conversion rates just to break even and much more sophisticated business models just to break even. And you have to change the economic window of your business over longer periods of time to compensate for the fact that most people don't buy initially. They buy later, they buy beyond 90 days to two years. You have to take all of that. And really, what our model of, if you want to think about our framework, how we think about digital marketing really is the thing that we do, that I think we do better than most other people do, and I don't, it's not a humble brag or that's just, I think it's an intellectually defensible statement, but that we understand we're willing to talk about the unspoken realities that many others are either unaware of, like the implications of them, or they're aware of, but they don't want to explicitly talk about it. So things like traffic costs are going up, conversion rates don't generally happen in the first 30 days that most of them, like these are all parts of reality. So when we look at all of reality and build methods that reflect that reality, like that makes sense, right? It's not, so a lot of, it doesn't mean that the other ways of doing direct response don't work or didn't work. It just means they're becoming harder and harder. And when you spend any time with a big direct response marketer, it's all the same story, which is it's getting harder and harder to get high quality traffic. There's more competition, people are yelling louder, the signal to noise ratio is dramatically in favor of noise. All of those things are true. And those truths are what, the implications of those truths are what inform what we teach in the system and how we approach marketing is that we recognize the implications of what's true and we create strategies that work with those realities, not fighting against them. I think that answers, I hope that answers the question. And yes, people are seeing this, absolutely seeing this. It's the biggest question or the biggest challenge that you'll hear when you get behind the scenes with any big direct response marketer or marketer companies in general. So what you're really trying to find, the lane is a lot of companies want a website for branding and marketing. And there are people who want to know how to manage their websites. Those are not always the same people and often they're not. So what you need to find is that overlap between needs a website, like if you think it's like a two-part Venn diagram, there are an enormous number of people that need branding and marketing website. And then there's an enormous number of people who want to learn how to update websites. But the overlap is a much smaller sliver of people who need both. And that's the part that you have to validate. Like how do you find, because it's really, and be cautious with it, because you're moving down the levels of economic possibility, frankly, that you're going to be finding people that money is a concern. I think you might, I assume that you have already validated that people will pay you to build their website. I think you want to sell the companion online course, my memory is correct from previous conversations, you're a WordPress developer. So I think you want to actually validate the getting the most from your WordPress site online course as a, and market that to, because any of a much larger audience, sell that to people who already have a website and they want to get more out of it and they want to do that work themselves. Because if you don't do that, it's really hard to understand which part of your business is that needs your attention, because if you can have people who are, they don't want to build a website right now, they want to work on their own website. So you're not attractive to them because your course is about both. And so when you separate them, you get a much, when you pull, especially the second part, when you pull the course out of the mix, and people can do the website and the companion course, or if they already have a website, they can, they can learn how to get the most from it. You get a much larger audience and a much truer signal to validate the offer. So that's, that's how I would think about it. Now you've added some notes here. That's something's clearly gone sideways there, even if you don't get any refunds. So if you just smashing those, those 90s, 85% open rates, I think that's a good sign and that's easy to see. And then the other things that Sean mentioned, if you have a community and things like that. All right, let's jump to the next one. I have a few popular services that can be executed on the cart. But after hiring me for one thing, a client may see me as a one stop shop. Would you suggest thinking about the experience for each individual service or thinking about the experience from the point of them hiring me for all the services I offer? That's actually a question that you have to ask yourself, because ultimately, what do you want? What do you want the customers to do? Because what you want customers to do should be the, that should answer the, that should be your guiding principle for will it make the boat go faster? In that case, let's just, I'm just going to make an assumption. Let's assume that what you really want is a full service client that pays you lots of money, loves everything you do for them. They're easy to work with, they appreciate you, and they don't churn, right? Like that's the perfect customer. Well, if that's, if that's the definition of success for your business throughput for your business is the rate at which you produce those customers. That's what tells you if you're making the boat go faster or not. So if you add 10 customers in a month for individual things, is your boat going any faster? No, because you've just added individuals that you say, you might say, well, half of the time when people buy one thing, they upgrade to, they do all of it within 30 days. Okay. So then you at least have some nuance. You're like, well, I don't know if it's making them, well, you can kind of say, yeah, it's contributing to making the boat go faster. I don't know which 50% will upgrade, but I do know consistently 50% will. Okay. Yeah, that activity is making the boat go faster. But the way to answer this question for yourself is to say, what do you want to be true and subordinate all decision making in your business to making that true? That's the game. Like whoever anonymous earlier asked, like the real, like the real way to do things, the real way to do things is to figure out what you're trying to accomplish for you and your customers and subordinate all decision making to that. That's the game. That's the whole, whole game that we're playing and you get to make the rules of this game. So if you're, if, if, if, perfect customer for you, is someone who hires you for everything, every decision you make should be to make that happen. And if the thing that you're doing doesn't make that happen, then you don't do it. Period. That's the implementation of will it make the boat go faster in action. That's the thinking that at the core, that's the thinking. Anything you want to throw at that, Andre? Now, if for some reason that was a fluke, and you're like, oh, just somebody introduced me to somebody and then the other person introduced me, okay, then got you. But I'm not trying to sound cute with this. But what I want to draw attention to is this idea. You've heard once a while that, hey, I did this thing and it worked so well. I stopped doing it. That's so many people on the internet. It's like, if I did something and it was here, I was kicking ass with it, but then I went over here and I built this other system. And I get it. I get why you're thinking about this. And there's great, there's so much nuance here. But if you have something that works, exhaust that first, not hold the exhaustive, but at least exhaust the easy 50%, like the easy to low hanging fruit. If you have five other people to talk to who could turn into five more 30,000 clients on the sit down, the opportunity cost of building a digital system instead is just too damn high. Go have those conversations. Do that work. Exhaust that because that is proven. And to be clear, and this is another nuance people need to understand. When we talk about proven offers, offers are proven in contexts. So to say, oh, I've sold this $30,000 high ticket item. I've sold it to two people. I've validated it. Now I'm going to go sell it online. You validated it selling it in person. You have not validated it selling it online. So if you, what you validated is a face to face conversation that sold a high ticket when in whatever context got you those prospects. So what I would suggest doing is continuing to validate it in that same way. Are there other people you could talk to? And maybe the answer is no. And I get the nuance that Jason's added here. He says, I can keep doing it, but I want to start leveraging marketing because it gets exhausting doing outreach. And that's smart thinking, but you need to find that inflection point. And it's this low hanging fruit idea. Think about it in terms of like units of energy. Okay, over the next 60 days, let's just say every day you have 10 units of energy. If my math is right, which is skeptical over the next 60 days, and we're going to have 600 units of energy. I think if I use those 600 units of energy in service, like I could use all of them pursuing customers the way I got the first two. I think that would make sense. But in the next, in the return on that, that time seems like it would be pretty high. The 60 days after that, I probably could, like at that point, it would be harder. I'd have to take more to get, and let's just make up a number. Let's say that 600 units of energy over 60 days, you're pretty confident you can get three clients. So it's 200 units of energy per client that you acquire. But when, because you've got some relationships, you're speaking out of an event and this and that, yeah, I think I could probably pull it off. But when you look at day 61 through 120, it's probably going to take three or 400 units of energy. I probably could get one or two clients in the next 60 days. So I don't really have the same resources. Well, that's the inflection point then. That's the part to say, you know what, I'm going to take the next 60 days, I'm going to spend 80% of my time focused on just making the donuts, getting these customers that I know I can get through a method I've proven. But the remaining 20%, I'm going to start on the strategy to port this over to a digital marketing version or about a less hands-on approach and start building the initial infrastructure. That's how I would think about this, because the answer to the question, what's likely to make the boat go faster or what will it make the boat go faster, could be either, right? But of the two, which one, then you get to the nuance of the question. You have two things you could do. Which of them do you feel is more likely in the next 60 days, which is a good planning profile, a good planning timeframe, which strategy is likely to really get you the result that you've already validated? Well, it's the in-person one. I would lean toward that. And it's not the canonical answer though. Like, you might say, hey, these two clients, they put some money in the bank for me.
